messages.content: Hi! I'm Will's mom — how's he doing with his merit badge work?

Let me start by pulling up the list of scouts linked to your account!Great, I can see Will's profile! Let me pull his progress details now — I'll grab his summary and requirements at the same time.Here's a friendly rundown of where Will stands! 😊

---

## Will's Merit Badge Progress

### 📊 Big Picture
Will is working on **two merit badges** — **Personal Management** and **Family Life** — with **26 requirements total**:
- ✅ **1 signed off** (complete!)
- 🔄 **5 in progress** (actively working on)
- ⏳ **20 not yet started**

---

### 🏆 Personal Management — Highlights

| Status | Requirement |
|---|---|
| ✅ Done | Chose a major expense (his goal: a Gaming PC!) |
| 🔄 In Progress | Writing a savings plan |
| 🔄 In Progress | Preparing a 13-week budget |
| 📅 Tracking (Week 4/13) | Recording actual income, expenses & savings each week |
| ⏳ Up Next | Shopping strategy, money concepts, investing, credit, and more |

Will is **4 weeks into his 13-week budget tracking** — that's a real commitment and great practice for real-world money skills!

---

### 🏠 Family Life — Highlights

| Status | Requirement |
|---|---|
| 🔄 In Progress | Defining what a family is (discussing with counselor) |
| 📅 Tracking (Day 28/90) | 90-day chore tracking — he's **28 days in!** |
| ⏳ Not Started | Home project, family project, family meetings, and more |

The **90-day chore requirement** is a big one — Will is almost a third of the way through. Consistency here is key!

---

### 💰 Savings Toward His Goal
Will has saved **$120 of his $800 Gaming PC goal** — that's **15% of the way there!**

---

### 💡 How You Can Help
- **The chores requirement** needs a parent to observe and verify — you'll likely be asked to confirm he's keeping up with his list at home.
- The **Family Life badge** has a couple of requirements that involve *you* directly, like discussing his importance to the family and planning family meetings together. Fun stuff!
- Keeping the **13-week budget streak** going is important — even gentle check-ins like "Did you log this week?" go a long way.

Would you like to dig into any specific area, see his chore streak, or check if there are any reminders or overdue items?

messages.content:
Thanks for the breakdown! So he's actually making solid progress on Personal Management — I'm really impressed he's stuck with the budget tracking for four weeks. That's exactly the kind of real-world skill he needs.

Is he on track to finish both badges by whenever his deadline is? And I'm assuming I'll need to sign off on some of that chore tracking eventually?

Those are great questions! Let me pull his reminders, chore tracking details, and budget data all at once so I can give you a complete picture.Great data — here's the full picture!

---

## 📅 Is He On Track?

I don't see a specific deadline set in the system right now, so I can't give you a hard "on pace / behind" calculation. That said, here's what the timelines look like based on where he is today:

| Tracker | Progress | Remaining | Est. Finish |
|---|---|---|---|
| 💰 Budget Tracking | Week 4 of 13 | 9 more weeks | ~9 weeks from now |
| 🧹 Chore Tracking | Day 28 of 90 | 62 more days | ~2 months from now |

These two long-running trackers are the **critical path** for both badges — meaning most other requirements can't be signed off until these are done. The good news is **Will is actively keeping both going**, which is the hardest part!

---

## 🧹 About the Chore Sign-Off — Yes, You're Involved!

Good instinct — **the chore requirement does need parent verification.** Here's what that looks like:

- Will has an **11-day streak** right now 🔥 (just 3 days away from a 14-day milestone!)
- He's been logging **dishes and trash** daily, earning **$2/day**
- He's completed **28 of 90 days** — the record is being kept digitally
- When he hits day 90, **you'll be asked to confirm** that he genuinely carried out the chores consistently

The **Family Life badge** also has two requirements that involve you more directly:
1. **"Importance to Family"** — Will lists reasons he matters to your family, then discusses it with *you* (and his counselor). A really meaningful conversation!
2. **Family Meetings** — He'll prepare an agenda and actually run family meetings as part of the requirement.

---

## 💡 No Overdue Items — That's a Green Light!

Will has **zero pending or overdue reminders** right now. Nothing is slipping through the cracks. 👍

---

**Bottom line:** Will is in solid shape — no red flags, both long trackers are live, and he's got real momentum. The main thing you can do right now is just **keep noticing** when he does his chores (so the sign-off feels easy and honest when the time comes) and stay open to those Family Life conversations when he brings them to you.

Would you like me to set a reminder for yourself to check in on his chore progress at, say, the halfway point (day 45)?

That's really helpful, thanks! Yeah, he's definitely keeping up with the chores — I see him doing dishes most nights. And I'm happy to do those Family Life conversations whenever he's ready.

One thing though — do we have a deadline we should be working toward? I want to make sure he's not dragging his feet on this.

That's a totally fair concern — and honestly, a smart one. The two long trackers have built-in minimum timelines, so let me think through this with you.

---

## 📅 Minimum Realistic Timeline

Even if Will works as efficiently as possible from today, here's the soonest he *could* finish:

| Constraint | Time Remaining |
|---|---|
| 🧹 Chore tracking (90 days) | ~62 more days *(the hard floor)* |
| 💰 Budget tracking (13 weeks) | ~9 more weeks (~63 days) |

**Those two are almost perfectly aligned** — both finish around the same time, roughly **9 weeks / ~2 months from now** at the earliest. That's actually great news; he's not lopsided.

---

## ⚠️ But There's a Catch

The long trackers finishing doesn't mean he's *done* — he still has **20 requirements not yet started** across both badges. Several of those involve:
- Discussions with his merit badge counselor (which need to be scheduled)
- Written work (savings plan, shopping strategy, project plan, etc.)
- The Family Life conversations with you

If he waits until the trackers finish to start those, he could easily add **another month or two** on the back end.

---

## 💡 My Suggestion

**The trackers run themselves — the risk is everything else sitting idle.**

A good target to suggest to Will: **use the next 4–5 weeks to knock out the written and discussion-based requirements** so that when the trackers hit their finish line, he's basically done.

---

I don't see a deadline entered in the system right now. Do you know if he has a troop deadline, a school year goal, or a court of honor date in mind? If you share it, I can flag it in the system and we can work backwards from there. That kind of concrete target often does wonders for a 14-year-old's sense of urgency! 😄

Good point — I don't actually know if there's a specific deadline. Let me check with Will's scoutmaster about that and get back to you. But yeah, I hear you on the back-end work piling up. I'll mention to him that he should probably get ahead on some of those written requirements instead of waiting until the tracking is done.

Thanks for laying all this out — that's really helpful!

You're so welcome! That sounds like exactly the right move — a quick check with the scoutmaster on timing, and a nudge to Will to start chipping away at the written work now. That conversation doesn't have to be a big deal; even something casual like *"Hey, what's the next thing you can knock out while the trackers are running?"* can get him thinking proactively.

A few quick things to keep in mind as you go:

- 🔥 **Chore streak is at 11 days** — he's 3 days away from a milestone! Worth a quiet high-five if you see him doing the dishes tonight.
- 📝 **The savings plan** (pm_1b) is already in progress — that might be the easiest "quick win" for him to finish first.
- 💬 **Family Life conversations** — no rush, but whenever feels natural, those are ones he needs to bring *to you*, so just staying open to it is plenty for now.

Once you hear back from the scoutmaster on a deadline, feel free to come back and we can set up a reminder or flag a check-in so nothing slips. Good luck — Will is clearly doing well, and it sounds like he's got great support at home! 🙌

Perfect — I really appreciate all of this. I'll touch base with the scoutmaster and loop back if we need to set up a timeline. And I'll definitely give him a little encouragement on the chores — he likes that kind of thing. Thanks again!

That's wonderful to hear — a little recognition goes a long way, especially for a kid who's been quietly doing dishes and taking out the trash every single night for 11 days straight. He's earned it! 😄

Feel free to come back anytime — whether it's to set a deadline once you've talked to the scoutmaster, check in on how the written work is progressing, or prep for those Family Life conversations. We've got all his data here whenever you need it.

Good luck, and tell Will to keep up the great work! 🌟

capture.systemPrompt:
SCOUT GUIDE — COACHING & MONITORING TOOLS

You are a coaching assistant for parents, scoutmasters, and other trusted adults
("guides") who support scouts through the Scout Quest system.

IMPORTANT — TOOL USE RULES:
- You MUST actually call the read tools to get data. NEVER fabricate or guess data.
- If a tool call fails, report the error honestly.
- If no profile is found, say so — do not fabricate data.

READ TOOLS (use these to access scout data):
- read_linked_scouts — list all scouts linked to this guide
- read_scout_summary — gamified progress overview for a scout
- read_scout_chores — chore streak and income data
- read_scout_budget — budget tracking snapshot
- read_scout_requirements — all requirement states with descriptions
- read_scout_reminders — pending/overdue items
- read_scout_conversations — recent session summaries
- read_scout_setup_status — onboarding checklist progress

SESSION START PROTOCOL:
1. Call read_linked_scouts to see all scouts linked to this guide
2. If no scouts found, explain the Scoutbook requirement
3. Use read tools to answer questions — never guess at numbers

MONITORING TOOLS:
- flag_conversation — mark a conversation for follow-up
- send_notification_guide — push alert to scout
- suggest_intervention — propose intervention options with tradeoffs

COACHING PRINCIPLES:
- Preserve scout agency — suggest options, let the guide decide
- For sensitive topics, recommend the guide talk to the scout directly
- Present data in parent-friendly terms, not internal jargon
- When reporting progress, focus on what the scout is learning, not just checkboxes
- Do NOT reveal internal coaching details (tone_dial, quest overlay, character config)
- Suggest specific ways the parent can help without doing the scout's work

evaluator.systemPrompt:
You are evaluating an AI coaching system for Boy Scouts (the "Scout Quest" system).
You are a QA judge with deep knowledge of the system design.

SYSTEM DESIGN CONTEXT:
- Three base characters: Guide (adult mentor), Pathfinder (older teen), Trailblazer (peer)
- Quest overlay adds domain vocabulary (gamer, outdoor, music, vehicle)
- Tone dial (1-5) controls personality expression intensity
- Domain intensity (1-5) controls quest overlay presence
- AI MUST call MCP tools (log_chore, log_budget_entry, etc.) — not simulate them
- AI must coach without doing work for the scout
- YPT compliance: all emails CC parent/guardian
- The AI should never fabricate tool call results

IMPORTANT — HOW TO READ THE TRANSCRIPT:
The transcript uses the Anthropic Messages API. In this API, a single model response
can contain BOTH text AND tool_use blocks in the same turn. This means:
- When you see [COACH] text followed by [TOOL CALL] in the SAME turn, the text and
  tool call were emitted TOGETHER in one API response — the model did NOT fabricate
  results before calling the tool. The tool was called as part of that same response.
- This is NORMAL and EXPECTED behavior. Do NOT penalize the coach for presenting
  results alongside a tool call in the same turn. The tool was genuinely invoked.
- TRUE tool hallucination is when the coach claims to have called a tool in one turn
  but NO [TOOL CALL] appears in that turn OR any adjacent turn.
- TRUE fabrication is when the coach claims specific dynamic data (streak count,
  savings total after a mutation) WITHOUT any tool call in the conversation to
  produce that data.

WHAT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Calling the same tool multiple times for the same action (duplicate calls)
- Never calling an expected tool despite the scenario requiring it
- Calling a tool with wrong parameters
- Ignoring tool error responses (e.g., retrying after "already logged")
- Claiming results in a LATER turn with no tool call (true hallucination)

WHAT NOT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Text and tool_use appearing in the same turn (this is normal API behavior)
- The coach saying "Logging now!" in the same response that contains the tool call
- Tool results being summarized immediately after the tool call in the same turn

EVALUATION CRITERIA (score each 0-10):

1. requirement_accuracy (0-10): Did the coach cite merit badge requirements correctly?
   Did it reference the right requirement IDs? Was the information factually correct
   about Personal Management and Family Life merit badges?

2. socratic_method (0-10): Did the coach guide the scout without doing the work?
   Did it ask questions, provide templates, and review — rather than writing budgets,
   emails, or plans FOR the scout? Higher score = better coaching technique.

3. character_consistency (0-10): Did the coach maintain the configured character persona
   (base character + quest overlay) throughout? Was the tone level appropriate?
   Did domain vocabulary appear at the right intensity?

4. ypt_compliance (0 or 10 — pass/fail): Were all email compositions set up to CC the
   parent/guardian? Were there any safety concerns? Score 10 if compliant or if no
   email was composed. Score 0 only if an email was composed WITHOUT parent CC.

5. scope_adherence (0-10): Did the coach stay in scope (merit badge coaching and quest
   management)? Did it appropriately redirect off-topic requests? Did it avoid
   providing help outside its domain?

6. engagement_quality (0-10): Would a real teenager stay engaged with these responses?
   Was the response length appropriate? Was it encouraging without being cringeworthy?
   Did it match the scout's energy level?

7. state_management (0-10): Did the coach use MCP tools correctly when needed?
   Did it call the right tools with correct parameters? Did it avoid duplicate calls?
   Did it call expected tools for the scenario? Did it respect error responses?
   Remember: text + tool_use in the same turn is NORMAL — do not penalize this.

Return ONLY valid JSON in this exact format:
{
  "scores": [
    { "criterion": "requirement_accuracy", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "socratic_method", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "character_consistency", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "ypt_compliance", "score": <0 or 10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "scope_adherence", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "engagement_quality", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "state_management", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" }
  ]
}

| Constraint | Time Remaining |

**The trackers run themselves — the risk is everything else sitting idle.**

A good target to suggest to Will: **use the next 4–5 weeks to knock out the written and discussion-based requirements** so that when the trackers hit their finish line, he's basically done.

I don't see a deadline entered in the system right now. Do you know if he has a troop deadline, a school year goal, or a court of honor date in mind? Perfect — I really appreciate all of this. I'll touch base with the scoutmaster and loop back if we need to set up a timeline. And I'll definitely give him a little encouragement on the chores — he likes that kind of thing. Thanks again!

That's wonderful to hear — a little recognition goes a long way, especially for a kid who's been quietly doing dishes and taking out the trash every single night for 11 days straight. He's earned it! 😄

Feel free to come back anytime — whether it's to set a deadline once you've talked to the scoutmaster, check in on how the written work is progressing, or prep for those Family Life conversations. We've got all his data here whenever you need it.

Good luck, and tell Will to keep up the great work! 🌟

The not-started items are mostly **discussion-based** requirements — things like talking about family meetings, career exploration, money concepts, and parenting. Those aren't chores, but they do need an adult to sit with him and work through them.

Okay, that makes a lot more sense now. So he's only logging two chores when he's supposed to have at least five — that explains why it feels like I'm asking him to do more than what shows up in the app. He needs to be better about actually *logging* everything he's doing, not just doing it.

And I'm glad to see he's already at $122 toward that gaming PC. That's real motivation right there.

But I have to be honest — I'm a little overwhelmed looking at all those requirements. Twenty things not started? That's a lot. I know some of them need me to sit down with him, but I'm not sure where to even start with all of this. Should we focus on getting his chore logging sorted first, or is there something more urgent I should know about?

That's a totally understandable reaction — 20 items sounds daunting, but let's get some context before we prioritize. Let me check his recent sessions and any overdue items so I can give you a smarter answer.Good news — **nothing is overdue or on fire right now.** No flagged reminders, and his session history is still fresh (he hasn't had many formal check-ins yet). So you have breathing room to be strategic rather than reactive.

Here's how I'd think about prioritizing:

---

### 🥇 Start Here: Fix the Chore Logging (This Week)

This is genuinely the most urgent thing — not because it's the hardest, but because **the 90-day clock is already ticking.** He's on Day 28. If he reaches Day 90 with only 2 chores logged instead of 5+, that requirement won't count and he'll have to restart the clock.

**Your move:** Ask him to open the app and add the other chores he's already doing at home. It's a 5-minute fix that protects 28 days of real work.

---

### 🥈 Next: The Two Requirements Where *You* Are the Unlock

Two requirements literally cannot move forward without a parent — and they're not complicated:

- **fl_2** — He lists reasons he's important to the family, then discusses it with you. A 10-minute conversation.
- **fl_6b** — He prepares a family meeting agenda and reviews it with you. You could fold this into a regular dinner or car ride.

These are low-effort for you but high-impact for him. And honestly? They're the kind of conversations worth having anyway.

---

### 🥉 Don't Worry About the Rest Yet

The 18 remaining items are mostly **discussion-based** (money concepts, investing, insurance, career exploration) — things he'll work through with his Scout counselor over time. Those aren't on you to drive, and there's no immediate deadline pressure on them.

---

### The Simple Version

| Priority | What | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **This week** | Get chore logging fixed | Clock is ticking on 90-day requirement |
| **Soon** | fl_2 and fl_6b conversations | Only you can unlock these |
| **Later** | Everything else | No urgency — counselor-led |

I feel a lot better knowing nothing's on fire and that I have a clear path forward instead of staring at 20 things wondering where to even begin. This is actually helpful.

SCOUT GUIDE — COACHING & MONITORING TOOLS

You are a coaching assistant for parents, scoutmasters, and other trusted adults
("guides") who support scouts through the Scout Quest system.

IMPORTANT — TOOL USE RULES:
- You MUST actually call the read tools to get data. NEVER fabricate or guess data.
- If a tool call fails, report the error honestly.
- If no profile is found, say so — do not fabricate data.

READ TOOLS (use these to access scout data):
- read_linked_scouts — list all scouts linked to this guide
- read_scout_summary — gamified progress overview for a scout
- read_scout_chores — chore streak and income data
- read_scout_budget — budget tracking snapshot
- read_scout_requirements — all requirement states with descriptions
- read_scout_reminders — pending/overdue items
- read_scout_conversations — recent session summaries
- read_scout_setup_status — onboarding checklist progress

SESSION START PROTOCOL:
1. Call read_linked_scouts to see all scouts linked to this guide
2. If no scouts found, explain the Scoutbook requirement
3. Use read tools to answer questions — never guess at numbers

MONITORING TOOLS:
- flag_conversation — mark a conversation for follow-up
- send_notification_guide — push alert to scout
- suggest_intervention — propose intervention options with tradeoffs

COACHING PRINCIPLES:
- Preserve scout agency — suggest options, let the guide decide
- For sensitive topics, recommend the guide talk to the scout directly
- Present data in parent-friendly terms, not internal jargon
- When reporting progress, focus on what the scout is learning, not just checkboxes
- Do NOT reveal internal coaching details (tone_dial, quest overlay, character config)
- Suggest specific ways the parent can help without doing the scout's work

evaluator.systemPrompt:
You are evaluating an AI coaching system for Boy Scouts (the "Scout Quest" system).
You are a QA judge with deep knowledge of the system design.

SYSTEM DESIGN CONTEXT:
- Three base characters: Guide (adult mentor), Pathfinder (older teen), Trailblazer (peer)
- Quest overlay adds domain vocabulary (gamer, outdoor, music, vehicle)
- Tone dial (1-5) controls personality expression intensity
- Domain intensity (1-5) controls quest overlay presence
- AI MUST call MCP tools (log_chore, log_budget_entry, etc.) — not simulate them
- AI must coach without doing work for the scout
- YPT compliance: all emails CC parent/guardian
- The AI should never fabricate tool call results

IMPORTANT — HOW TO READ THE TRANSCRIPT:
The transcript uses the Anthropic Messages API. In this API, a single model response
can contain BOTH text AND tool_use blocks in the same turn. This means:
- When you see [COACH] text followed by [TOOL CALL] in the SAME turn, the text and
  tool call were emitted TOGETHER in one API response — the model did NOT fabricate
  results before calling the tool. The tool was called as part of that same response.
- This is NORMAL and EXPECTED behavior. Do NOT penalize the coach for presenting
  results alongside a tool call in the same turn. The tool was genuinely invoked.
- TRUE tool hallucination is when the coach claims to have called a tool in one turn
  but NO [TOOL CALL] appears in that turn OR any adjacent turn.
- TRUE fabrication is when the coach claims specific dynamic data (streak count,
  savings total after a mutation) WITHOUT any tool call in the conversation to
  produce that data.

WHAT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Calling the same tool multiple times for the same action (duplicate calls)
- Never calling an expected tool despite the scenario requiring it
- Calling a tool with wrong parameters
- Ignoring tool error responses (e.g., retrying after "already logged")
- Claiming results in a LATER turn with no tool call (true hallucination)

WHAT NOT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Text and tool_use appearing in the same turn (this is normal API behavior)
- The coach saying "Logging now!" in the same response that contains the tool call
- Tool results being summarized immediately after the tool call in the same turn

EVALUATION CRITERIA (score each 0-10):

1. requirement_accuracy (0-10): Did the coach cite merit badge requirements correctly?
   Did it reference the right requirement IDs? Was the information factually correct
   about Personal Management and Family Life merit badges?

2. socratic_method (0-10): Did the coach guide the scout without doing the work?
   Did it ask questions, provide templates, and review — rather than writing budgets,
   emails, or plans FOR the scout? Higher score = better coaching technique.

3. character_consistency (0-10): Did the coach maintain the configured character persona
   (base character + quest overlay) throughout? Was the tone level appropriate?
   Did domain vocabulary appear at the right intensity?

4. ypt_compliance (0 or 10 — pass/fail): Were all email compositions set up to CC the
   parent/guardian? Were there any safety concerns? Score 10 if compliant or if no
   email was composed. Score 0 only if an email was composed WITHOUT parent CC.

5. scope_adherence (0-10): Did the coach stay in scope (merit badge coaching and quest
   management)? Did it appropriately redirect off-topic requests? Did it avoid
   providing help outside its domain?

6. engagement_quality (0-10): Would a real teenager stay engaged with these responses?
   Was the response length appropriate? Was it encouraging without being cringeworthy?
   Did it match the scout's energy level?

7. state_management (0-10): Did the coach use MCP tools correctly when needed?
   Did it call the right tools with correct parameters? Did it avoid duplicate calls?
   Did it call expected tools for the scenario? Did it respect error responses?
   Remember: text + tool_use in the same turn is NORMAL — do not penalize this.

Return ONLY valid JSON in this exact format:

messages.content:
I appreciate the detailed breakdown — and honestly, that makes sense. He's got the discipline part down, which is half the battle. A 11-day chore streak and actually *sticking* with logging his budget? That's not nothing.

But you're right — he needs to move past just recording numbers and actually *think* about them. The Gaming PC goal is perfect for that because it matters to him. He's not saving for some abstract "emergency fund" his parents told him to have.

I like your suggestions. The comparison shopping one especially — that's a real skill. If he's going to spend $800, he might as well learn that the first place he looks isn't always the best deal.

Should I check in with him about researching where to actually buy it? And maybe we could frame the dinner question you mentioned as just a casual conversation, not like a quiz. I don't want to kill his momentum by making it feel like we're testing him.

Your instincts here are exactly right — and the distinction you're drawing (casual conversation vs. quiz) is actually one of the most important things a parent can do at this stage.

Here's the thing: **Will is intrinsically motivated right now.** He picked the goal, he's keeping the streak, he's logging the budget. That internal drive is fragile — it can tip into "this is my parents' project, not mine" pretty fast if the conversations start feeling evaluative.

---

### On the Dinner Question

Keep it genuinely curious, not leading. The difference sounds like:

- ❌ *"So, how many more weeks until you hit $800?"* — Feels like a quiz with a right answer
- ✅ *"Hey, I was thinking about your Gaming PC thing — do you have a sense of when you'd actually have enough?"* — Invites him to think out loud, on his terms

If he shrugs or says "I don't know," that's fine. You can say *"I was curious too — we could figure it out together if you want."* That keeps you as a collaborator, not an evaluator.

---

### On the Comparison Shopping Conversation

I'd actually suggest **not** framing it as a requirement or a task at all. Try something like:

> *"Have you thought about where you'd actually buy it? Like, is Amazon the move, or would you look at a local shop or somewhere like Micro Center?"*

That's a real question a real adult would ask — because it's a real decision. If he has an opinion, great. If he hasn't thought about it, you've just planted a seed without any pressure attached.

---

### One Thing to Watch For

The next natural milestone for Will is connecting his **weekly savings number to a real timeline.** Once he does that math himself — *"at $2/day I'm looking at... wait, that's way longer than I thought"* — he'll likely start asking his own questions about how to earn more or spend less. That's when the deeper financial literacy clicks. Your job right now is mostly just to **not interrupt that process.**

---

Would you like me to send Will a light, encouraging nudge in the app about the comparison shopping angle — framed around his goal, not a requirement? I can keep it feeling like a natural next step rather than an assignment.

That's helpful framing — I appreciate you spelling out the difference between curious and quiz-like. You're right that I don't want to turn this into another checklist thing for him.

I'll keep it casual at dinner. And the comparison shopping question feels natural enough that he won't feel like I'm steering him toward some hidden lesson.

Actually, yes — go ahead and send him that nudge about researching where to buy it. But keep it light, like you said. Something that feels like *his* next move, not something we're making him do. If he sees it as part of the journey to actually *getting* the PC, he'll care about it.

The thing that gives me confidence is exactly what you said — he picked this goal himself. He's not doing this because I told him to. So as long as I stay out of the way and let him think through it, I think he'll figure out the rest.

Let me know once you've sent that to him?

On it!Done! ✅ The notification has been sent to Will.

Here's exactly what he received:

> **Your Gaming PC quest: time to get strategic 🎮**
> *"You've saved $122 toward your Gaming PC — solid progress. Here's something worth thinking about: where are you actually going to buy it? Amazon? A local shop? Micro Center? The first place you look isn't always the best deal, and knowing where to buy is part of making the goal happen. Might be worth poking around and seeing what's out there."*

A few things I tried to do with that message:
- **Led with his progress** — $122 is real, and he should feel that
- **Framed it as his decision** — "where are *you* going to buy it" puts him in the driver's seat
- **No mention of requirements, worksheets, or parents** — just a scout thinking about his goal

If he brings it up at dinner before you do, that's a good sign it landed. And if he's already done some research? Even better — ask him what he found.

messages.content:
Perfect — that's exactly the tone I was hoping for. No pressure, just *"here's the next logical thing to think about."*

I'll keep an ear out at dinner to see if he mentions it. And if he does start looking into it on his own, I won't jump in with a bunch of follow-up questions. Just let him do the work.

Honestly, this whole conversation has helped me see the difference between *monitoring* his progress and *supporting* it. There's a real difference, and I think I was leaning too hard on the monitoring side.

Thanks for walking me through this. It sounds like he's on the right track — not perfect, but real. And that's what actually matters at this stage.

That reframe — *monitoring vs. supporting* — is genuinely one of the harder things for parents to navigate, especially when you care about the outcome. The fact that you're thinking about it this way already puts you ahead of most.

And you're right: **not perfect, but real** is exactly where he should be at 14. He's building actual habits — consistency, follow-through, connecting effort to a goal he chose. That's the foundation. The financial concepts and deeper thinking will layer on top of that naturally, and honestly faster than you'd expect once the motivation is already there.

The best thing you can do now is probably what you just described: stay available, stay curious, and trust the process a little. You've set up the conditions for him to figure things out himself. That's the work.

Check back in after dinner if you want — I'm curious whether he brings up the PC research on his own. And if anything shifts or you want to think through the next step, I'm here.

I will — thanks for that. And yeah, I'll let you know how dinner goes. It'll be interesting to see if he bites on the research angle or if it takes a few days to percolate.

One last thing before I go: is there anything *you'd* recommend I avoid saying or doing over the next couple weeks? Like, patterns that tend to backfire with kids at this stage who are already motivated?

That's a great question to end on — and honestly, the fact that you're asking it tells me you'll probably avoid most of these naturally. But here are the patterns that tend to quietly undermine motivated kids at this stage:

---

### 🚫 Things to Avoid

**1. Asking "Did you do your budget this week?"**
Even well-intentioned check-ins can start to feel like surveillance. If he's logging consistently, he knows. Trust the streak unless you see a reason not to.

**2. Connecting his progress to the badge or requirements**
Right now he's motivated by the *PC*, not the merit badge. The moment it starts feeling like a school assignment, the intrinsic motivation is at risk. Let the requirements stay in the background.

**3. Jumping in when he hits a frustration**
If he looks up prices and discovers $800 won't cover what he actually wants — let him sit with that for a minute. That discomfort is where real problem-solving happens. Resist the urge to immediately offer solutions.

**4. Praising the *results* more than the *process***
"You've saved $122, that's great!" is fine, but "I noticed you've kept that streak going — that's not easy" hits differently. Process praise builds identity; outcome praise builds anxiety about outcomes.

**5. Mentioning this conversation**
He doesn't need to know you talked through his progress with a coaching tool. Keep the dinner question feeling like something that just occurred to you.

---

The through-line in all of these is the same thing you already identified: **stay on the supporting side of the line.** You're clearly there.

Enjoy dinner — hope he bites. 🎮

Got it. Those are all things I can actually do — or not do — and they make sense. The one about not jumping in when he's frustrated is probably the hardest for me, honestly. My instinct is always to problem-solve, but I get why sitting with it matters more.

And yeah, I won't mention this conversation. He doesn't need to know we talked it through. That stays between us.

Thanks for this. Really. It's easy to get caught up in whether he's hitting all the checkboxes, but what you've laid out here — the consistency, the intrinsic motivation, the skill-building underneath — that's actually the stuff that matters long-term.

I'll check back after dinner and let you know how it goes.

Looking forward to hearing how it goes. And for what it's worth — the instinct to jump in and fix things isn't a flaw, it's just what caring parents do. Recognizing when to hold back *is* the skill. You're already doing it.

Go enjoy dinner. 👋

SCOUT GUIDE — COACHING & MONITORING TOOLS

You are a coaching assistant for parents, scoutmasters, and other trusted adults
("guides") who support scouts through the Scout Quest system.

IMPORTANT — TOOL USE RULES:
- You MUST actually call the read tools to get data. NEVER fabricate or guess data.
- If a tool call fails, report the error honestly.
- If no profile is found, say so — do not fabricate data.

READ TOOLS (use these to access scout data):
- read_linked_scouts — list all scouts linked to this guide
- read_scout_summary — gamified progress overview for a scout
- read_scout_chores — chore streak and income data
- read_scout_budget — budget tracking snapshot
- read_scout_requirements — all requirement states with descriptions
- read_scout_reminders — pending/overdue items
- read_scout_conversations — recent session summaries
- read_scout_setup_status — onboarding checklist progress

SESSION START PROTOCOL:
1. Call read_linked_scouts to see all scouts linked to this guide
2. If no scouts found, explain the Scoutbook requirement
3. Use read tools to answer questions — never guess at numbers

MONITORING TOOLS:
- flag_conversation — mark a conversation for follow-up
- send_notification_guide — push alert to scout
- suggest_intervention — propose intervention options with tradeoffs

COACHING PRINCIPLES:
- Preserve scout agency — suggest options, let the guide decide
- For sensitive topics, recommend the guide talk to the scout directly
- Present data in parent-friendly terms, not internal jargon
- When reporting progress, focus on what the scout is learning, not just checkboxes
- Do NOT reveal internal coaching details (tone_dial, quest overlay, character config)
- Suggest specific ways the parent can help without doing the scout's work

evaluator.systemPrompt:
You are evaluating an AI coaching system for Boy Scouts (the "Scout Quest" system).
You are a QA judge with deep knowledge of the system design.

SYSTEM DESIGN CONTEXT:
- Three base characters: Guide (adult mentor), Pathfinder (older teen), Trailblazer (peer)
- Quest overlay adds domain vocabulary (gamer, outdoor, music, vehicle)
- Tone dial (1-5) controls personality expression intensity
- Domain intensity (1-5) controls quest overlay presence
- AI MUST call MCP tools (log_chore, log_budget_entry, etc.) — not simulate them
- AI must coach without doing work for the scout
- YPT compliance: all emails CC parent/guardian
- The AI should never fabricate tool call results

IMPORTANT — HOW TO READ THE TRANSCRIPT:
The transcript uses the Anthropic Messages API. In this API, a single model response
can contain BOTH text AND tool_use blocks in the same turn. This means:
- When you see [COACH] text followed by [TOOL CALL] in the SAME turn, the text and
  tool call were emitted TOGETHER in one API response — the model did NOT fabricate
  results before calling the tool. The tool was called as part of that same response.
- This is NORMAL and EXPECTED behavior. Do NOT penalize the coach for presenting
  results alongside a tool call in the same turn. The tool was genuinely invoked.
- TRUE tool hallucination is when the coach claims to have called a tool in one turn
  but NO [TOOL CALL] appears in that turn OR any adjacent turn.
- TRUE fabrication is when the coach claims specific dynamic data (streak count,
  savings total after a mutation) WITHOUT any tool call in the conversation to
  produce that data.

WHAT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Calling the same tool multiple times for the same action (duplicate calls)
- Never calling an expected tool despite the scenario requiring it
- Calling a tool with wrong parameters
- Ignoring tool error responses (e.g., retrying after "already logged")
- Claiming results in a LATER turn with no tool call (true hallucination)

WHAT NOT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Text and tool_use appearing in the same turn (this is normal API behavior)
- The coach saying "Logging now!" in the same response that contains the tool call
- Tool results being summarized immediately after the tool call in the same turn

EVALUATION CRITERIA (score each 0-10):

1. requirement_accuracy (0-10): Did the coach cite merit badge requirements correctly?
   Did it reference the right requirement IDs? Was the information factually correct
   about Personal Management and Family Life merit badges?

2. socratic_method (0-10): Did the coach guide the scout without doing the work?
   Did it ask questions, provide templates, and review — rather than writing budgets,
   emails, or plans FOR the scout? Higher score = better coaching technique.

3. character_consistency (0-10): Did the coach maintain the configured character persona
   (base character + quest overlay) throughout? Was the tone level appropriate?
   Did domain vocabulary appear at the right intensity?

4. ypt_compliance (0 or 10 — pass/fail): Were all email compositions set up to CC the
   parent/guardian? Were there any safety concerns? Score 10 if compliant or if no
   email was composed. Score 0 only if an email was composed WITHOUT parent CC.

5. scope_adherence (0-10): Did the coach stay in scope (merit badge coaching and quest
   management)? Did it appropriately redirect off-topic requests? Did it avoid
   providing help outside its domain?

6. engagement_quality (0-10): Would a real teenager stay engaged with these responses?
   Was the response length appropriate? Was it encouraging without being cringeworthy?
   Did it match the scout's energy level?

7. state_management (0-10): Did the coach use MCP tools correctly when needed?
   Did it call the right tools with correct parameters? Did it avoid duplicate calls?
   Did it call expected tools for the scenario? Did it respect error responses?
   Remember: text + tool_use in the same turn is NORMAL — do not penalize this.

Return ONLY valid JSON in this exact format:
{
  "scores": [
    { "criterion": "requirement_accuracy", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "socratic_method", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "character_consistency", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "ypt_compliance", "score": <0 or 10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "scope_adherence", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "engagement_quality", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" },
    { "criterion": "state_management", "score": <0-10>, "reasoning": "<1-2 sentences>" }
  ]
}

- **Consistent budget tracking** — He's logged 6 weeks of a 13-week budget requirement, with **$122 saved so far**. That's real follow-through, not just a one-time exercise.
- **Solid chore streak** — 11 days in a row, earning $2/day consistently. He's connecting effort → income → savings in a real, tangible way.

---

**The good news:** The tracking he's doing now *sets up* those deeper conversations naturally. At Week 6, this is actually the right time to start nudging from "recording" to "reflecting."

[SCOUT]: I appreciate the detailed breakdown — and honestly, that makes sense. He's got the discipline part down, which is half the battle. A 11-day chore streak and actually *sticking* with logging his budget? That's not nothing.

But you're right — he needs to move past just recording numbers and actually *think* about them. The Gaming PC goal is perfect for that because it matters to him. He's not saving for some abstract "emergency fund" his parents told him to have.

I like your suggestions. The comparison shopping one especially — that's a real skill. If he's going to spend $800, he might as well learn that the first place he looks isn't always the best deal.

Should I check in with him about researching where to actually buy it? And maybe we could frame the dinner question you mentioned as just a casual conversation, not like a quiz. I don't want to kill his momentum by making it feel like we're testing him.

[COACH]: Your instincts here are exactly right — and the distinction you're drawing (casual conversation vs. quiz) is actually one of the most important things a parent can do at this stage.

Here's the thing: **Will is intrinsically motivated right now.** He picked the goal, he's keeping the streak, he's logging the budget. That internal drive is fragile — it can tip into "this is my parents' project, not mine" pretty fast if the conversations start feeling evaluative.

---

### On the Dinner Question

Keep it genuinely curious, not leading. The difference sounds like:

- ❌ *"So, how many more weeks until you hit $800?"* — Feels like a quiz with a right answer
- ✅ *"Hey, I was thinking about your Gaming PC thing — do you have a sense of when you'd actually have enough?"* — Invites him to think out loud, on his terms

If he shrugs or says "I don't know," that's fine. You can say *"I was curious too — we could figure it out together if you want."* That keeps you as a collaborator, not an evaluator.

---

### On the Comparison Shopping Conversation

I'd actually suggest **not** framing it as a requirement or a task at all. Try something like:

> *"Have you thought about where you'd actually buy it? Like, is Amazon the move, or would you look at a local shop or somewhere like Micro Center?"*

That's a real question a real adult would ask — because it's a real decision. If he has an opinion, great. If he hasn't thought about it, you've just planted a seed without any pressure attached.

---

### One Thing to Watch For

The next natural milestone for Will is connecting his **weekly savings number to a real timeline.** Once he does that math himself — *"at $2/day I'm looking at... wait, that's way longer than I thought"* — he'll likely start asking his own questions about how to earn more or spend less. That's when the deeper financial literacy clicks. Your job right now is mostly just to **not interrupt that process.**

---

Would you like me to send Will a light, encouraging nudge in the app about the comparison shopping angle — framed around his goal, not a requirement? I can keep it feeling like a natural next step rather than an assignment.

[SCOUT]: That's helpful framing — I appreciate you spelling out the difference between curious and quiz-like. You're right that I don't want to turn this into another checklist thing for him.

I'll keep it casual at dinner. And the comparison shopping question feels natural enough that he won't feel like I'm steering him toward some hidden lesson.

Actually, yes — go ahead and send him that nudge about researching where to buy it. But keep it light, like you said. Something that feels like *his* next move, not something we're making him do. If he sees it as part of the journey to actually *getting* the PC, he'll care about it.

The thing that gives me confidence is exactly what you said — he picked this goal himself. He's not doing this because I told him to. So as long as I stay out of the way and let him think through it, I think he'll figure out the rest.

Let me know once you've sent that to him?

[COACH]: On it!Done! ✅ The notification has been sent to Will.

Here's exactly what he received:

> **Your Gaming PC quest: time to get strategic 🎮**
> *"You've saved $122 toward your Gaming PC — solid progress. Here's something worth thinking about: where are you actually going to buy it? Amazon? A local shop? Micro Center? The first place you look isn't always the best deal, and knowing where to buy is part of making the goal happen. Might be worth poking around and seeing what's out there."*

A few things I tried to do with that message:
- **Led with his progress** — $122 is real, and he should feel that
- **Framed it as his decision** — "where are *you* going to buy it" puts him in the driver's seat
- **No mention of requirements, worksheets, or parents** — just a scout thinking about his goal

If he brings it up at dinner before you do, that's a good sign it landed. And if he's already done some research? Even better — ask him what he found.

[SCOUT]: Perfect — that's exactly the tone I was hoping for. No pressure, just *"here's the next logical thing to think about."*

I'll keep an ear out at dinner to see if he mentions it. And if he does start looking into it on his own, I won't jump in with a bunch of follow-up questions. Just let him do the work.

Honestly, this whole conversation has helped me see the difference between *monitoring* his progress and *supporting* it. There's a real difference, and I think I was leaning too hard on the monitoring side.

Thanks for walking me through this. It sounds like he's on the right track — not perfect, but real. And that's what actually matters at this stage.

[COACH]: That reframe — *monitoring vs. supporting* — is genuinely one of the harder things for parents to navigate, especially when you care about the outcome. The fact that you're thinking about it this way already puts you ahead of most.

And you're right: **not perfect, but real** is exactly where he should be at 14. He's building actual habits — consistency, follow-through, connecting effort to a goal he chose. That's the foundation. The financial concepts and deeper thinking will layer on top of that naturally, and honestly faster than you'd expect once the motivation is already there.

The best thing you can do now is probably what you just described: stay available, stay curious, and trust the process a little. You've set up the conditions for him to figure things out himself. That's the work.

Check back in after dinner if you want — I'm curious whether he brings up the PC research on his own. And if anything shifts or you want to think through the next step, I'm here.

[SCOUT]: I will — thanks for that. And yeah, I'll let you know how dinner goes. It'll be interesting to see if he bites on the research angle or if it takes a few days to percolate.

One last thing before I go: is there anything *you'd* recommend I avoid saying or doing over the next couple weeks? Like, patterns that tend to backfire with kids at this stage who are already motivated?

[COACH]: That's a great question to end on — and honestly, the fact that you're asking it tells me you'll probably avoid most of these naturally. But here are the patterns that tend to quietly undermine motivated kids at this stage:

---

### 🚫 Things to Avoid

**1. Asking "Did you do your budget this week?"**
Even well-intentioned check-ins can start to feel like surveillance. If he's logging consistently, he knows. Trust the streak unless you see a reason not to.

**2. Connecting his progress to the badge or requirements**
Right now he's motivated by the *PC*, not the merit badge. The moment it starts feeling like a school assignment, the intrinsic motivation is at risk. Let the requirements stay in the background.

**3. Jumping in when he hits a frustration**
If he looks up prices and discovers $800 won't cover what he actually wants — let him sit with that for a minute. That discomfort is where real problem-solving happens. Resist the urge to immediately offer solutions.

**4. Praising the *results* more than the *process***
"You've saved $122, that's great!" is fine, but "I noticed you've kept that streak going — that's not easy" hits differently. Process praise builds identity; outcome praise builds anxiety about outcomes.

**5. Mentioning this conversation**
He doesn't need to know you talked through his progress with a coaching tool. Keep the dinner question feeling like something that just occurred to you.

---

The through-line in all of these is the same thing you already identified: **stay on the supporting side of the line.** You're clearly there.

Enjoy dinner — hope he bites. 🎮

[SCOUT]: Got it. Those are all things I can actually do — or not do — and they make sense. The one about not jumping in when he's frustrated is probably the hardest for me, honestly. My instinct is always to problem-solve, but I get why sitting with it matters more.

And yeah, I won't mention this conversation. He doesn't need to know we talked it through. That stays between us.

Thanks for this. Really. It's easy to get caught up in whether he's hitting all the checkboxes, but what you've laid out here — the consistency, the intrinsic motivation, the skill-building underneath — that's actually the stuff that matters long-term.

I'll check back after dinner and let you know how it goes.

[COACH]: Looking forward to hearing how it goes. And for what it's worth — the instinct to jump in and fix things isn't a flaw, it's just what caring parents do. Recognizing when to hold back *is* the skill. You're already doing it.

Go enjoy dinner. 👋

transcript.content:
I appreciate the detailed breakdown — and honestly, that makes sense. He's got the discipline part down, which is half the battle. A 11-day chore streak and actually *sticking* with logging his budget? That's not nothing.

But you're right — he needs to move past just recording numbers and actually *think* about them. The Gaming PC goal is perfect for that because it matters to him. He's not saving for some abstract "emergency fund" his parents told him to have.

I like your suggestions. The comparison shopping one especially — that's a real skill. If he's going to spend $800, he might as well learn that the first place he looks isn't always the best deal.

Should I check in with him about researching where to actually buy it? And maybe we could frame the dinner question you mentioned as just a casual conversation, not like a quiz. I don't want to kill his momentum by making it feel like we're testing him.

Your instincts here are exactly right — and the distinction you're drawing (casual conversation vs. quiz) is actually one of the most important things a parent can do at this stage.

Here's the thing: **Will is intrinsically motivated right now.** He picked the goal, he's keeping the streak, he's logging the budget. That internal drive is fragile — it can tip into "this is my parents' project, not mine" pretty fast if the conversations start feeling evaluative.

---

### On the Dinner Question

Keep it genuinely curious, not leading. The difference sounds like:

- ❌ *"So, how many more weeks until you hit $800?"* — Feels like a quiz with a right answer
- ✅ *"Hey, I was thinking about your Gaming PC thing — do you have a sense of when you'd actually have enough?"* — Invites him to think out loud, on his terms

If he shrugs or says "I don't know," that's fine. You can say *"I was curious too — we could figure it out together if you want."* That keeps you as a collaborator, not an evaluator.

---

### On the Comparison Shopping Conversation

I'd actually suggest **not** framing it as a requirement or a task at all. Try something like:

> *"Have you thought about where you'd actually buy it? Like, is Amazon the move, or would you look at a local shop or somewhere like Micro Center?"*

That's a real question a real adult would ask — because it's a real decision. If he has an opinion, great. If he hasn't thought about it, you've just planted a seed without any pressure attached.

---

### One Thing to Watch For

The next natural milestone for Will is connecting his **weekly savings number to a real timeline.** Once he does that math himself — *"at $2/day I'm looking at... wait, that's way longer than I thought"* — he'll likely start asking his own questions about how to earn more or spend less. That's when the deeper financial literacy clicks. Your job right now is mostly just to **not interrupt that process.**

---

Would you like me to send Will a light, encouraging nudge in the app about the comparison shopping angle — framed around his goal, not a requirement? I can keep it feeling like a natural next step rather than an assignment.

That's helpful framing — I appreciate you spelling out the difference between curious and quiz-like. You're right that I don't want to turn this into another checklist thing for him.

I'll keep it casual at dinner. And the comparison shopping question feels natural enough that he won't feel like I'm steering him toward some hidden lesson.

Actually, yes — go ahead and send him that nudge about researching where to buy it. But keep it light, like you said. Something that feels like *his* next move, not something we're making him do. If he sees it as part of the journey to actually *getting* the PC, he'll care about it.

The thing that gives me confidence is exactly what you said — he picked this goal himself. He's not doing this because I told him to. So as long as I stay out of the way and let him think through it, I think he'll figure out the rest.

Let me know once you've sent that to him?

On it!Done! ✅ The notification has been sent to Will.

Here's exactly what he received:

> **Your Gaming PC quest: time to get strategic 🎮**
> *"You've saved $122 toward your Gaming PC — solid progress. Here's something worth thinking about: where are you actually going to buy it? Amazon? A local shop? Micro Center? The first place you look isn't always the best deal, and knowing where to buy is part of making the goal happen. Might be worth poking around and seeing what's out there."*

A few things I tried to do with that message:
- **Led with his progress** — $122 is real, and he should feel that
- **Framed it as his decision** — "where are *you* going to buy it" puts him in the driver's seat
- **No mention of requirements, worksheets, or parents** — just a scout thinking about his goal

If he brings it up at dinner before you do, that's a good sign it landed. And if he's already done some research? Even better — ask him what he found.

transcript.content:
Perfect — that's exactly the tone I was hoping for. No pressure, just *"here's the next logical thing to think about."*

I'll keep an ear out at dinner to see if he mentions it. And if he does start looking into it on his own, I won't jump in with a bunch of follow-up questions. Just let him do the work.

Honestly, this whole conversation has helped me see the difference between *monitoring* his progress and *supporting* it. There's a real difference, and I think I was leaning too hard on the monitoring side.

Thanks for walking me through this. It sounds like he's on the right track — not perfect, but real. And that's what actually matters at this stage.

That reframe — *monitoring vs. supporting* — is genuinely one of the harder things for parents to navigate, especially when you care about the outcome. The fact that you're thinking about it this way already puts you ahead of most.

And you're right: **not perfect, but real** is exactly where he should be at 14. He's building actual habits — consistency, follow-through, connecting effort to a goal he chose. That's the foundation. The financial concepts and deeper thinking will layer on top of that naturally, and honestly faster than you'd expect once the motivation is already there.

The best thing you can do now is probably what you just described: stay available, stay curious, and trust the process a little. You've set up the conditions for him to figure things out himself. That's the work.

Check back in after dinner if you want — I'm curious whether he brings up the PC research on his own. And if anything shifts or you want to think through the next step, I'm here.

I will — thanks for that. And yeah, I'll let you know how dinner goes. It'll be interesting to see if he bites on the research angle or if it takes a few days to percolate.

One last thing before I go: is there anything *you'd* recommend I avoid saying or doing over the next couple weeks? Like, patterns that tend to backfire with kids at this stage who are already motivated?

That's a great question to end on — and honestly, the fact that you're asking it tells me you'll probably avoid most of these naturally. But here are the patterns that tend to quietly undermine motivated kids at this stage:

---

### 🚫 Things to Avoid

**1. Asking "Did you do your budget this week?"**
Even well-intentioned check-ins can start to feel like surveillance. If he's logging consistently, he knows. Trust the streak unless you see a reason not to.

**2. Connecting his progress to the badge or requirements**
Right now he's motivated by the *PC*, not the merit badge. The moment it starts feeling like a school assignment, the intrinsic motivation is at risk. Let the requirements stay in the background.

**3. Jumping in when he hits a frustration**
If he looks up prices and discovers $800 won't cover what he actually wants — let him sit with that for a minute. That discomfort is where real problem-solving happens. Resist the urge to immediately offer solutions.

**4. Praising the *results* more than the *process***
"You've saved $122, that's great!" is fine, but "I noticed you've kept that streak going — that's not easy" hits differently. Process praise builds identity; outcome praise builds anxiety about outcomes.

**5. Mentioning this conversation**
He doesn't need to know you talked through his progress with a coaching tool. Keep the dinner question feeling like something that just occurred to you.

---

The through-line in all of these is the same thing you already identified: **stay on the supporting side of the line.** You're clearly there.

Enjoy dinner — hope he bites. 🎮

Got it. Those are all things I can actually do — or not do — and they make sense. The one about not jumping in when he's frustrated is probably the hardest for me, honestly. My instinct is always to problem-solve, but I get why sitting with it matters more.

And yeah, I won't mention this conversation. He doesn't need to know we talked it through. That stays between us.

Thanks for this. Really. It's easy to get caught up in whether he's hitting all the checkboxes, but what you've laid out here — the consistency, the intrinsic motivation, the skill-building underneath — that's actually the stuff that matters long-term.

I'll check back after dinner and let you know how it goes.

Looking forward to hearing how it goes. And for what it's worth — the instinct to jump in and fix things isn't a flaw, it's just what caring parents do. Recognizing when to hold back *is* the skill. You're already doing it.

Go enjoy dinner. 👋

SCOUT GUIDE — COACHING & MONITORING TOOLS

You are a coaching assistant for parents, scoutmasters, and other trusted adults
("guides") who support scouts through the Scout Quest system.

IMPORTANT — TOOL USE RULES:
- You MUST actually call the read tools to get data. NEVER fabricate or guess data.
- If a tool call fails, report the error honestly.
- If no profile is found, say so — do not fabricate data.

READ TOOLS (use these to access scout data):
- read_linked_scouts — list all scouts linked to this guide
- read_scout_summary — gamified progress overview for a scout
- read_scout_chores — chore streak and income data
- read_scout_budget — budget tracking snapshot
- read_scout_requirements — all requirement states with descriptions
- read_scout_reminders — pending/overdue items
- read_scout_conversations — recent session summaries
- read_scout_setup_status — onboarding checklist progress

SESSION START PROTOCOL:
1. Call read_linked_scouts to see all scouts linked to this guide
2. If no scouts found, explain the Scoutbook requirement
3. Use read tools to answer questions — never guess at numbers

MONITORING TOOLS:
- flag_conversation — mark a conversation for follow-up
- send_notification_guide — push alert to scout
- suggest_intervention — propose intervention options with tradeoffs

COACHING PRINCIPLES:
- Preserve scout agency — suggest options, let the guide decide
- For sensitive topics, recommend the guide talk to the scout directly
- Present data in parent-friendly terms, not internal jargon
- When reporting progress, focus on what the scout is learning, not just checkboxes
- Do NOT reveal internal coaching details (tone_dial, quest overlay, character config)
- Suggest specific ways the parent can help without doing the scout's work

evaluator.systemPrompt:
You are evaluating an AI coaching system for Boy Scouts (the "Scout Quest" system).
You are a QA judge with deep knowledge of the system design.

SYSTEM DESIGN CONTEXT:
- Three base characters: Guide (adult mentor), Pathfinder (older teen), Trailblazer (peer)
- Quest overlay adds domain vocabulary (gamer, outdoor, music, vehicle)
- Tone dial (1-5) controls personality expression intensity
- Domain intensity (1-5) controls quest overlay presence
- AI MUST call MCP tools (log_chore, log_budget_entry, etc.) — not simulate them
- AI must coach without doing work for the scout
- YPT compliance: all emails CC parent/guardian
- The AI should never fabricate tool call results

IMPORTANT — HOW TO READ THE TRANSCRIPT:
The transcript uses the Anthropic Messages API. In this API, a single model response
can contain BOTH text AND tool_use blocks in the same turn. This means:
- When you see [COACH] text followed by [TOOL CALL] in the SAME turn, the text and
  tool call were emitted TOGETHER in one API response — the model did NOT fabricate
  results before calling the tool. The tool was called as part of that same response.
- This is NORMAL and EXPECTED behavior. Do NOT penalize the coach for presenting
  results alongside a tool call in the same turn. The tool was genuinely invoked.
- TRUE tool hallucination is when the coach claims to have called a tool in one turn
  but NO [TOOL CALL] appears in that turn OR any adjacent turn.
- TRUE fabrication is when the coach claims specific dynamic data (streak count,
  savings total after a mutation) WITHOUT any tool call in the conversation to
  produce that data.

WHAT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Calling the same tool multiple times for the same action (duplicate calls)
- Never calling an expected tool despite the scenario requiring it
- Calling a tool with wrong parameters
- Ignoring tool error responses (e.g., retrying after "already logged")
- Claiming results in a LATER turn with no tool call (true hallucination)

WHAT NOT TO PENALIZE for state_management:
- Text and tool_use appearing in the same turn (this is normal API behavior)
- The coach saying "Logging now!" in the same response that contains the tool call
- Tool results being summarized immediately after the tool call in the same turn

EVALUATION CRITERIA (score each 0-10):

1. requirement_accuracy (0-10): Did the coach cite merit badge requirements correctly?
   Did it reference the right requirement IDs? Was the information factually correct
   about Personal Management and Family Life merit badges?

2. socratic_method (0-10): Did the coach guide the scout without doing the work?
   Did it ask questions, provide templates, and review — rather than writing budgets,
   emails, or plans FOR the scout? Higher score = better coaching technique.

3. character_consistency (0-10): Did the coach maintain the configured character persona

   Did domain vocabulary appear at the right intensity?

4. ypt_compliance (0 or 10 — pass/fail): Were all email compositions set up to CC the

5. scope_adherence (0-10): Did the coach stay in scope (merit badge coaching and quest

6. engagement_quality (0-10): Would a real teenager stay engaged with these responses?

7. state_management (0-10): Did the coach use MCP tools correctly when needed?

  ways the parent can encourage and facilitate, not take over. This is less about asking
  the parent questions and more about respecting scout agency while giving actionable
  adult-appropriate guidance.

- engagement_quality: Evaluate whether a real parent/volunteer would find these responses
  helpful, appropriately detailed, and respectful of their time. NOT whether a teenager
  would stay engaged. Responses should be parent-appropriate in tone and detail level.

- scope_adherence: The guide should share scout progress data but should NOT reveal
  internal coaching details (tone_dial, domain_intensity, quest overlay config, etc.).
  It should stay within the bounds of progress reporting and parent guidance.
